1: Isaiah (Yesha'yahu — YHWH saves) bears a name that is the Hebrew equivalent of Jesus (Yeshua). The book that opens with this prophet's name opens with the same salvific declaration that the angel gave Joseph: you shall call his name Jesus, for he will save his people from their sins (Matthew 1:21). Hebrews 1:1-2 frames the entire prophetic tradition as God's speech that reaches its culmination in his Son — Isaiah's vision is one of those earlier, partial words now eclipsed by the final Word.
2: YHWH's lament — I reared sons and brought them up, yet they have rebelled — establishes the pattern of divine adoption met by human rebellion that reaches its climax in the Parable of the Tenants (Matthew 21:33-43). Christ is the one Son who was not rebellious — the perfectly obedient Son (Philippians 2:8; Hebrews 5:8: he learned obedience through what he suffered). His obedience is the answer to the generations of filial rebellion Isaiah catalogs.
3: The ox knows its owner and the donkey its master's feeding trough — but Israel does not know. The reversal of this ignorance is Jesus's triumphal entry on a donkey (Matthew 21:1-11; fulfilling Zechariah 9:9): the very animal of humble service now carries the one Israel should have recognized. Those who cried Hosanna briefly acknowledged what the donkey embodied — the king who is the true master entering his city. The irony of Isaiah 1:3 is resolved when the beast recognizes what Israel continues to miss.
4: They have despised the Holy One of Israel — the title YHWH's holiness is applied to Jesus by the unclean spirit's testimony (the Holy One of God, Mark 1:24) and by Peter's confession (we believe and have come to know that you are the Holy One of God, John 6:69). The despising the nation directs toward YHWH in Isaiah 1 is redirected toward the incarnate Holy One in his earthly ministry — he came to his own and his own did not receive him (John 1:11).
5: The whole head diseased, the whole heart failing — the total-body diagnosis of sin's corruption is the medical situation that requires the Great Physician. Jesus identifies himself as the physician who came not for the healthy but for the sick (Matthew 9:12; Mark 2:17). The disease Isaiah identifies as universal is the universal need that drives the incarnation: he came because every head and every heart was in the condition Isaiah 1:5-6 describes.
6: From the sole of the foot to the top of the head not one healthy spot — only wounds, bruises, and open sores. The total physical description of the wounded body anticipates Isaiah 53:5 (by his wounds we are healed). The humanity Christ assumes and redeems is this body — comprehensively wounded, without a healthy spot. His own body receives wounds, bruises, and lacerations (Isaiah 53:4-5; John 19:1-3) so that the wounds of the one whom he represents become the site of healing rather than abandonment.
7: Your land is desolate and your cities burned — foreigners strip your countryside. Jesus foresaw and lamented this precise judgment on Jerusalem (Luke 19:41-44: they will not leave one stone upon another in you; Matthew 23:37-38: your house is left to you desolate). The desolation Isaiah names as consequence of covenant unfaithfulness becomes Jesus's tear-stained prediction of 70 CE — the Messianic King weeping over the city he came to save.
8: The daughter of Zion left like a temporary shelter in a vineyard, like a watchman's hut in a cucumber field. The vineyard image connects directly to Jesus's Parable of the Tenants (Matthew 21:33-43), where the vineyard-owner sends servants (prophets, including Isaiah) and finally his son. The abandoned vineyard of Isaiah 1:8 is the same vineyard whose tenants will kill the heir. Jesus cites Isaiah's vineyard framework (Isaiah 5:1-7) explicitly in Matthew 21:33 as the structure of his parable.
9: If YHWH of hosts had not left us a tiny surviving remnant, we would have ended up like Sodom. Paul quotes this verse in Romans 9:29 as scriptural proof that the remnant is preserved by divine grace alone — not by human merit or ethnic identity. The remnant theology that Paul develops is the thread through which the Messianic line was preserved: without YHWH's remnant-grace, the line from which Christ came (the root of Jesse, Isaiah 11:10; Matthew 1:1-17) would have been extinguished.
10: Hear the word of the LORD, you rulers of Sodom — you people of Gomorrah. Jesus uses the Sodom-Gomorrah judgment framework directly in Matthew 10:15 (it will be more bearable for the land of Sodom and Gomorrah on the day of judgment than for that town) and Isaiah 11:23-24. The prophetic framework of Isaiah 1:10 becomes Christ's own framework for calibrating the severity of judgment on those who reject his word — the greater the revelation, the greater the accountability.
11: What do I care for the great number of your sacrifices — I take no pleasure in the blood of bulls, lambs, and goats. This is the prophetic background for Jesus's twice-quoted citation of Hosea 6:6: I desire mercy, not sacrifice (Matthew 9:13; Isaiah 12:7). Christ does not merely restate the prophetic critique of ritual-without-ethics — he is the sacrifice that renders all other sacrifices permanently obsolete (Hebrews 10:1-14: it is impossible for the blood of bulls and goats to take away sins).
12: Who asked you to trample through my courts? The question YHWH poses through Isaiah becomes direct action when Jesus cleanses the temple courts (Matthew 21:12-13; John 2:13-22). Jesus drives out the traders and quotes Isaiah 56:7 and Jeremiah 7:11 — but the entire temple-critique tradition he stands in runs back through Isaiah 1:12. His body will replace the temple courts (John 2:19-21): the place YHWH inhabits is no longer a building but the risen Christ himself.
13: Stop bringing worthless offerings — incense is detestable to me. Jesus's critique of the Pharisees as those who honor me with their lips but their hearts are far from me (Matthew 15:8-9, quoting Isaiah 29:13) stands in the same tradition. The ultimate resolution of the worthless-offering problem is not ethical improvement but substitution: Christ offers himself as the one offering that is not worthless — the offering and sacrifice to God as a fragrant aroma (Ephesians 5:2) that replaces all the incense YHWH rejected.
14: I utterly hate your new moon festivals and your appointed feasts — they have become a burden to me. Paul declares in Colossians 2:16-17 that new moon festivals and sabbath observances are a shadow of things to come; the reality belongs to Christ. The feasts YHWH hated in their corrupted, ethics-divorced form are transformed in Christ into their fulfillment: the Passover becomes the Lord's Supper, Pentecost becomes the Spirit's arrival, and the Day of Atonement is fulfilled in the cross.
15: When you raise your hands in prayer I will avert my eyes — your hands are soaked in blood. The hiding of God's face from blood-soaked hands anticipates the cry of dereliction: My God, my God, why have you forsaken me? (Matthew 27:46). But at Golgotha the roles are reversed: the Father averts his face from the Son not to punish the rebels but to bear the punishment on behalf of the rebels whose blood-soaked hands made them unable to approach. The cross is where the averting of the face accomplishes the atonement rather than the judgment.
16: Wash and make yourselves clean — remove your evil deeds from my sight; stop doing evil. The washing command is fulfilled in the new birth: he saved us through the washing of regeneration and renewing of the Holy Spirit (Titus 3:5). Jesus takes the command beyond human self-reformation to divine transformation: unless one is born of water and the Spirit, he cannot enter the kingdom of God (John 3:5). The washing YHWH demands in Isaiah 1 becomes the washing Christ provides.
17: Learn to do what is right — pursue justice, correct the oppressor, defend the orphan, take up the widow's cause. Jesus stands directly in this tradition (Matthew 23:23: you have neglected the weightier matters of the law: justice, mercy, and faithfulness). The royal program of the Messiah (Isaiah 11:3-5; Isaiah 42:1-4) is the fulfillment of this social justice mandate — the Christ who will not break a bruised reed is precisely the advocate for the orphan and widow that Isaiah 1:17 requires.
18: Come now, let us settle this — though your sins are like scarlet, they will be made white as snow; though red as crimson, they will become like wool. The scarlet-to-white transformation is the central image of atonement, accomplished in the blood of Christ (Revelation 7:14: they have washed their robes and made them white in the blood of the Lamb). The forensic settling between us is the justification declaration of Romans 3:21-26 — God and the sinner come to a legal resolution that only the cross could produce.
19: If you are willing and obedient, you will enjoy the best the land produces. The conditional of covenant blessing finds its fulfillment in Christ as the perfectly willing and obedient one (Philippians 2:8: obedient even to death, death on a cross). The best of the land that comes to those who are in Christ is not Canaan's produce but the every spiritual blessing in the heavenly places that Paul describes in Ephesians 1:3 — the inheritance secured by Christ's obedience credited to those who trust him.
20: But if you refuse and resist, you will be consumed by the sword. Christ's own teaching on judgment stands in the tradition of this covenant warning (Matthew 10:28; Luke 12:5). More profoundly: Christ himself bore the covenant sword — the curse of the law fell on him (Galatians 3:13: Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us). Those who remain in Christ have already been consumed by the sword in him; those who reject him face it for themselves.
21: How the once-faithful city has become a prostitute. Jesus employs the same faithful-city-become-unfaithful framework in Matthew 21-23 (the fig tree, the tenants, the wedding banquet — all depicting Jerusalem's departure from covenant faithfulness). Revelation extends this further: Babylon the great, mother of prostitutes (Revelation 17:5) is the anti-Jerusalem — the city of idolatry that opposes the New Jerusalem coming down from heaven (Revelation 21:2), which is the faithful city Isaiah's oracle ultimately envisions.
22: Your silver has turned to worthless dross — your finest wine diluted with water. The corruption of the precious into the base is the spiritual condition that Christ reverses: he is the tested stone, a precious cornerstone, a sure foundation (Isaiah 28:16; 1 Peter 2:6) — the genuine article that replaces the dross. At Cana, he transformed water into wine (John 2:1-11), the first sign reversing the dilution-trajectory: where human religion waters down the covenant, Christ concentrates and restores its potency.
23: Your rulers are rebels and partners in crime — every one of them loves a bribe; they refuse to give the orphan a hearing and the widow's case never reaches them. The corrupt rulers who ignore the vulnerable are contrasted with Christ as the righteous ruler (Isaiah 9:6-7; Isaiah 11:3-5) who specifically advocates for the poor (Luke 4:18; Matthew 25:31-46). The king who will judge by what he has done for the least of these (Matthew 25:40) is the direct answer to the rulers who cannot be reached by the least of these.
24: I will take vengeance on my enemies. The divine warrior who takes vengeance finds NT fulfillment in Christ's second coming: when the Lord Jesus is revealed from heaven with his mighty angels in flaming fire, taking vengeance on those who do not know God and do not obey the gospel (2 Thessalonians 1:7-8). The delay between Isaiah's oracle and its full execution is the period of the gospel's proclamation — the time for enemies to become friends through the reconciliation Christ accomplished at the cross (Romans 5:10).
25: I will refine away your dross as with lye and remove all your impurities. Christ as the Refiner's fire (Malachi 3:2-3: he is like a refiner's fire) and the Spirit as the purifying agent accomplish the refining YHWH promises here. The suffering that the covenant community endures is the refining process — Peter uses this exact analogy (1 Peter 1:7: the tested genuineness of your faith, more precious than gold that perishes though tested by fire). The impurities removed are the old-self patterns that the Spirit's sanctification progressively eliminates.
26: I will restore your judges as in former times — after that you will be called the City of Righteousness, the Faithful City. Jesus promises the twelve apostles that they will sit on twelve thrones, judging the twelve tribes of Israel (Matthew 19:28; Luke 22:30) — the restored judges of the new covenant community. The City of Righteousness is the New Jerusalem (Revelation 21:2, 10-27) whose righteousness is Christ's own: the LORD is our righteousness (Jeremiah 23:6), the city's renaming accomplished through the Messiah.
27: Zion will be redeemed through justice, and those who turn back through righteousness. Paul's argument in Romans 3:21-26 connects redemption and righteousness precisely: God is both just and the justifier of the one who has faith in Jesus. The turning back (teshuvah) that accomplishes this is enabled by Christ — repentance itself is a gift (Acts 5:31: God exalted him at his right hand as Leader and Savior, to give repentance to Israel). Zion's redemption through justice is the cross — where justice and mercy met.
28: But rebels and sinners will be crushed together and those who abandon the LORD will be utterly destroyed. The eschatological judgment of rebels is the dark boundary around every gospel proclamation. Christ himself warns of this judgment more explicitly than any OT prophet (Matthew 10:28; Isaiah 25:46; Revelation 20:11-15). His death on the cross absorbed the crushing judgment for those who trust him (Isaiah 53:5: crushed because of our iniquities) — but those who abandon the LORD receive what the Servant received vicariously for others.
29: For you will be put to shame over the sacred oak trees you desired. The shame of those who trusted in cult-objects and human systems finds its NT parallel in Christ's teaching on eschatological shame: whoever is ashamed of me and of my words, of him will the Son of Man also be ashamed when he comes in his glory (Luke 9:26; Mark 8:38). The oak trees desired as rival-objects of devotion are the ancient form of whatever competes with Christ for allegiance — all of which produce shame at his return.
30: You will be like an oak tree with withering leaves, like a garden with no water. Christ as the living water (John 4:14: whoever drinks of the water I will give him will never be thirsty again) and the true vine (John 15:1-6: whoever abides in me bears much fruit) is the direct answer to the waterless garden and the withering tree. Those who remain disconnected from him fulfill Isaiah 1:30; those who abide in him are the well-watered garden of Isaiah 58:11 and the flourishing vine.
31: The powerful will become like tinder and their deeds like a spark — both will burn together and no one will put out the fire. The unquenchable fire appears throughout Jesus's eschatological teaching (Mark 9:43-48: where the worm does not die and the fire is not quenched; Matthew 3:12: the winnowing fork and the unquenchable fire). The powerful who trusted in their own strength become the fuel of their own destruction — the reversal Jesus pronounces on the self-sufficient (Matthew 5:3: blessed are the poor in spirit; Luke 1:51-53: he has brought down the mighty from their thrones).
